Then, by continuity, there are neighborhoods U and V of x and y respectively with V /follows ∗ U (that is, every alternative in V is ranked strictly above every alternative in U according to /followsequal ∗ ). Because x /followsequal y , by local strictness, there exists ( x ′ , y ′ ) ∈ U × V with x ′ /follows y ′ . By continuity again there are neighborhoods U ′ /owner x ′ and V ′ /owner y ′ with U ′ /follows V ′ . Thus ( y ′ , x ′ ) ∈ ( V ∩ V ′ ) × ( U ∩ U ′ ) ⊆ /follows ∗ \ /follows . Second, consider the case where there is x, y ∈ X with x /followsequal ∗ y but ( x, y ) / ∈ /followsequal . This means that y /follows x , so there are neighborhoods U /owner x and V /owner y with V /follows X . By local strictness, there is ( x ′ , y ′ ) ∈ U × V with x ′ /follows ∗ y ′ . Thus ( y ′ , x ′ ) ∈ /followsequal \ /followsequal ∗ and we are in the situation above.

Let q ( x, y ) be a short notation for q ( /followsequal ∗ ; x, y ) , and for a binary relation R , let 1 R ( x, y ) if and only if ( x, y ) ∈ R . We show that µ ( /followsequal ∗ ) -µ ( /followsequal ) &gt; 0 by the sequence

Lemma 8. The mapping /followsequal ↦→ µ ( /followsequal ) is continuous on P .

Proof. Let /followsequal ∈ P and { /followsorequal i } i ∈ N be a sequence of preferences in P with /followsorequal i → /followsequal . If x /followsorequal i y for infinitely many values of i , then x /followsequal y . Hence, by completeness of the preference /followsequal , if the sequence of binary numbers { 1 ( x,y ) ∈ /followsorequal i } i ∈ N diverges, then x ∼ y .

Next, suppose x /notsimilar y . Then, there are two possibilities. Either 1 ( x,y ) ∈ /followsorequal i = 1 for all i large enough, then x /follows y . Or 1 ( x,y ) ∈ /followsorequal i = 0 for all i large enough, then, by completeness, 1 ( y,x ) ∈ /followsorequal i = 1 for all i large enough, and so y /follows x .

Finally, recall that, by Assumption (3), the set { ( x, y ) : x ∼ y } has λ -probability 0. Hence,

where the interchange between the limit and integration operators follows from Lebesgue dominated convergence. /square

Lemma 9. For all /followsequal /star ∈ P and all n ∈ N , the mapping /followsequal ↦→ ¯ d n ( /followsequal , R n ) defined on P is almost surely continuous at /followsequal /star .

Proof. Let /followsequal ∈ P and { /followsorequal i } i ∈ N be a sequence of preferences in P with /followsorequal i → /followsequal as i →∞ . Suppose that, for all decision problems { x k , y k } ∈ Σ n , x k /notsimilar y k . By the same argument as in Lemma 8, either x k /followsorequal i y k for i large enough and then x k /follows y k , or y k /followsorequal i x k for i large enough and then y k /follows x k . Hence, ¯ d n ( /followsorequal i , R n ) → ¯ d n ( /followsequal , R n ) as i →∞ . And by assumption (3), with probability 1, it is the case that for all decision problems { x k , y k } ∈ Σ n , x k /notsimilar y k , which concludes the proof. /square

Recall that, by Assumption (2), P is closed, and so compact as a closed subset of the set of all continuous binary relations on X , which itself is compact by Lemma 6 in Appendix B. That P is compact and the continuity statement in Lemma 9 imply that ¯ d n ( /followsequal , R n ) converges uniformly in probability to µ ( /followsequal ) over the domain P by uniform laws of large numbers (for example, Lemma 2.4 of Newey and McFadden (1994)). Finally, the mapping /followsequal ↦→ µ ( /followsequal ) is continuous on P by Lemma 8, and is uniquely maximized at /followsequal ∗ . Under the above conditions, standard consistency theorems apply, such as Theorem 2.4 of Newey and McFadden (1994), and thus {/followsequal n } n ∈ N converges to /followsequal ∗ in probability.

Appendix E. Proof of Proposition 1

Proposition 1 follows from the following result by Border and Segal (1994).

Theorem 4 (Theorem 8 of Border and Segal, 1994) . Let X be a locally compact and separable metric space. Let the space of continuous preference relations on X be endowed with the topology of closed convergence. Let the space of continuous functions on X be endowed with the topology of compact convergence. If Φ( u ) is locally strict, then Φ is continuous at u .

Under the conditions stated in Proposition 1, Φ is continuous on U by Theorem 4, so Φ( U ) is compact as the continuous image of a compact set, and hence meets Assumption (2).

## Appendix F. Proof of Corollary 3

As explained in Section 4.2, any nonconstant expected utility preference can be represented by a member of V ≡ { u ∈ R d : ‖ u ‖ = 1 and ∑ i u i = 0 } , which is a compact set in the Euclidean topology (recall that ‖·‖ refers to the Euclidean norm).

Second, each nonconstant expected utility preference is locally strict. Indeed, take p, p ′ ∈ ∆ d -1 and suppose that v · p ≥ v · p ′ . Let p ∗ , p ′ ∗ ∈ ∆ d -1 for which v · p ∗ &gt; v · p ′ ∗

First, observe that the set of functions { U v : v ∈ V} , where U v is defined as U v ( p ) = v · p , is sequentially compact in the topology of compact convergence. Indeed, let { U v n } n ∈ N be a sequence of functions where v n ∈ V , and let { v n k } k ∈ N be a convergent subsequence that converges to v ∗ . Then | U v n k ( p ) -U v ∗ ( p ) | = | ( v n k -v ∗ ) · p | ≤ √ ‖ v n k -v ∗ ‖‖ p ‖ ≤ √ ‖ v n k -v ∗ ‖ , where the first inequality is a Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. Because ∆ d -1 is compact, in the present case the topology of compact convergence is a metric topology, and the notions of compactness and sequential compactness are equivalent, so { U v : v ∈ V} is compact.

(such a pair exists because v represents a nonconstant preference). Then, for any α ∈ (0 , 1) , v · ( αp ∗ + (1 -α ) p ) &gt; v · ( αp ′ ∗ + (1 -α ) p ′ ) . Local strictness follows by choosing α arbitrarily small.

Corollary 3 then follows from applying Proposition 1.

## Appendix G. Proof of Corollary 4

The proof proceeds by computing an asymptotic lower bound on r ( η ) defined in Equation (1) in Section 3.2 and then applying Theorem 3.

Recall that X is the simplex ∆ d -1 , and P is the set of nonconstant expected utility preferences. It will be convenient to refer to the elements of the simplex by the generic symbols for alternatives x and y , as opposed to p and p ′ . Throughout, each preference of P is associated to a unique normalized vector of utility indices in the set { v ∈ R d : ∑ d i =1 v i = 0 , ‖ v ‖ = 1 } . For x ∈ ∆ d -1 and ε &gt; 0 , we let B ε ( x ) be the open ball of radius ε and center x in { z ∈ R d : ∑ d i =1 z i = 1 } , which is the affine span of the simplex ∆ d -1 .

## Appendix H. Proof of Lemma 2

Let {/followsequal n } n ∈ N be a converging sequence of Grodal-transitive preferences that are strictly monotone with respect to /triangleright , and let /followsequal ∗ be the limiting binary relation.

Suppose by means of contradiction that /followsequal ∗ is not strictly monotone with respect to /triangleright . In that case, there are x, y ∈ X for which x /triangleright y and yet y /followsequal ∗ x . Let { x n } n ∈ N , { y n } n ∈ N be any sequences of alternatives in X that converge to x and y and respectively and for which y n /followsequal n x n for all n (existence of sequences satisfying this property follows from the definition of closed convergence in Section 3). Because /triangleright is open, for n large enough, x n /triangleright y n , which contradicts the fact that /followsequal n is strictly monotone with respect to /triangleright . Hence, /followsequal ∗ is strictly monotone.

Recall that, by Lemma 6 in Appendix B, the set of continuous binary relations is closed, and so /followsequal ∗ is continuous. Also, for each x, y ∈ X , either x /followsequal n y or y /followsequal n x , so there is a subsequence {/followsequal n k } k ∈ N for which either x /followsequal n k y for all k , or for which y /followsequal n k x for all k . Hence, either x /followsequal y or y /followsequal x , which makes /followsequal ∗ complete. Hence, /followsequal ∗ is a preference.

Finally, we show that /followsequal ∗ is Grodal-transitive. Suppose x, y, z, w ∈ X satisfy x /followsequal ∗ y /follows ∗ z /followsequal ∗ w . Let { x n } n ∈ N , { y n } n ∈ N , { z n } n ∈ N , { w n } n ∈ N be sequences of alternatives in X that converge to x, y, z, w respectively, and for which x n /followsequal n y n and z n /followsequal n w n (which, again, exist by the definition of closed convergence). Since y /follows ∗ z , for n large enough, y n /follows n z n . Consequently, for n large, x n /followsequal n y n /follows n z n /followsequal n w n , which, by Grodal-transitivity, implies x n /followsequal n w n , and so x /followsequal ∗ w .

## Appendix I. Proof of Lemma 3

For any x ∈ X , let U x be the set { y : y /follows x } . Let us show that for each x, y ∈ X , either U x ⊆ U y , or U y ⊆ U x . To see this, suppose by means of contradiction that there is z ∈ U x \ U y and w ∈ U y \ U x . Then we have y /followsequal z /follows x and x /followsequal w /follows y . Therefore, x /followsequal w /follows y /followsequal z , which implies x /followsequal z by Grodal-transitivity. This contradicts z /follows x .

Now, fix x, y ∈ X such that x /followsequal y , and fix a neighborhood V of ( x, y ) in the product space X × X . By the lemma hypotheses, there exists ( x ′ , y ′ ) ∈ V such that x ′ /triangleright x and y /triangleright y ′ . Then, there are two possibilities: either either U y ′ ⊆ U x , or U x ⊆ U y ′ . In the first case, by monotonicity, y ′ /follows y , which implies y /follows x , contradicting x /followsequal y . So, we must have U x ⊆ U y ′ . Then x ′ ∈ U x , so x ′ ∈ U y ′ , which implies x ′ /follows y ′ . Hence, /followsequal is locally strict.

## Appendix J. Proof of Corollary 6

First, we prove that the set of alternatives M meets Assumption (1).

That M is locally compact follows from Theorem 1.8.3 of Schneider (1993), which demonstrates that the set of nonempty, convex subsets of the simplex is compact, and Theorem 18.4 of Willard (2004). The fact that M is separable obtains from Theorem 3.85(3) of Aliprantis and Border (2006), together with the fact that a subset of a separable metric space is itself separable (Problem 16G Part 1 of Willard, 2004). Next, we show that M is completely metrizable. This space is, by definition, metrizable. The Hausdorff metric is complete on the set of nonempty, closed, convex subsets of ∆ d -1 ; let us call this set M ∗ . This fact owes to a straightforward adaptation of Theorem 1.8.2 of Schneider (1993), together with the fact that M ∗ is a Hausdorffclosed set in the space of compact, convex, nonempty subsets of { x ∈ R d : ∑ i x i = 1 } , because a closed subset of a metric space is complete (Theorem 24.10 of Willard, 2004). Further, M is a Hausdorff (relatively) open subset of M ∗ . It then follows by Alexandroff's Theorem (Theorem 24.12 of Willard, 2004) that M is completely metrizable.

Secondly, we show that the hypothesis of Lemma 2 is satisfied, that is, that the dominance relation /squareoriginal is open.

This result can be obtained by means of a standard isometry between the set M endowed with the Hausdorff metric, and the set of support functions of members of M defined on U (Lemma 8 of Dekel, Lipman, and Rustichini, 2001; p. 594 of Dekel, Lipman, Rustichini, and Sarver, 2007; Theorem 1.8.11 of Schneider, 1993). For a member m of M , such a support function is written h m ( u ) = sup p ∈ m u · p . We endow the set of these support functions with the sup-norm metric. 23 Observe that m A /squareoriginal m B if and only if for every u ∈ U , h m A ( u ) &gt; h m B ( u ) . In particular, since U is compact, m A /squareoriginal m B if and only if there is ε &gt; 0 for which h m A ( u ) -h m B ( u ) &gt; ε .

For an element m of M , let B δ ( m ) denote the open ball of radius δ centered on m . Suppose m A , m B ∈ M satisfy m A /squareoriginal m B . Let m ′ A ∈ B ε 3 ( m A ) and m ′ B ∈ B ε 3 ( m B ) . By the isometry aforementioned, for each u ∈ U , h A ′ ( u ) &gt; h A ( u ) -ε 3 and h B ′ ( u ) &lt; h B ( u ) + ε 3 . Then h A ′ ( u ) -h B ′ ( u ) &gt; h A ( u ) -h B ( u ) -2 ε 3 &gt; ε 3 &gt; 0 , so that m ′ A /squareoriginal m ′ B . Hence /squareoriginal is open.

Thirdly, we show that the hypothesis of Lemma 3 is satisfied. Let m A ∈ M . For any α ∈ (0 , 1) , let m B be the Minkowski sum of m A and ∆ d -1 , weighted by α and 1 -α respectively, i.e., m B = { αp + (1 -α ) p ′ : p ∈ m A , p ′ ∈ ∆( X ) } . Then, m B is

23 So that the distance between two functions f, g is given by sup u ∈U | f ( u ) -g ( u ) | .

arbitrarily close to m A by choosing α small enough, and m B /squareoriginal m A . Similarly, fix p 0 in the interior of m A , and for any α ∈ (0 , 1) , let m C = αp 0 +(1 -α ) m A . Then, m C is arbitrarily close to m A by choosing α small enough, and m A /squareoriginal m C .

Hence, M meets Assumption (1), and by Lemmas 2 and 3, the class P meets Assumption (2).
